title: Cystic fibrosis: pancreatic enzyme replacement
vignette: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother due to chronic diarrhea and failure to gain weight appropriately. He has a history of recurrent respiratory infections, and prior sputum cultures have been positive for Pseudomonas aeruginosa. His younger brother died from a severe respiratory infection at age 9. Which of the following agents is most likely to improve this patient's condition in the long term?

clues.note: Chronic diarrhea (steatorrhea) with failure to thrive reflects fat/protein malabsorption from exocrine pancreatic insufficiency — a core feature of cystic fibrosis.

clues.text: sputum cultures have been positive for Pseudomonas aeruginosa
clues.note: Recurrent Pseudomonas aeruginosa respiratory infection in a child/teen is highly characteristic of cystic fibrosis (thick airway mucus, chronic colonization).

clues.text: younger brother died from a severe respiratory infection at age 9
clues.note: A sibling death from respiratory infection suggests an autosomal recessive lung disease — cystic fibrosis (CFTR mutation). Family history supports the inherited diagnosis.

whatMatters: Recurrent Pseudomonas lung infections + steatorrhea/failure to thrive + an affected sibling = cystic fibrosis with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. The long-term agent that improves the malabsorption/failure-to-thrive is pancreatic enzyme (lipase) replacement, which restores fat/nutrient and fat-soluble vitamin absorption.
strategy: For cystic fibrosis GI symptoms, target the pancreatic insufficiency: thick secretions block pancreatic ducts → enzyme deficiency → malabsorption. The fix is pancreatic enzyme replacement (lipase) plus fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). Don't treat it as an infectious or inflammatory bowel problem.

eliminations.label: antimotility agents — symptomatic, don't fix malabsorption
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: antimotility drugs (loperamide) slow gut transit to reduce stool frequency. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: they don't correct the underlying enzyme deficiency/malabsorption causing steatorrhea and failure to thrive. ✅ Correct if: the goal were short-term symptomatic control of nonmalabsorptive diarrhea.

eliminations.label: gluten-free diet — that's celiac disease
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a gluten-free diet treats celiac disease (gluten-triggered villous atrophy). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this child's malabsorption is from CF pancreatic insufficiency, not gluten sensitivity; respiratory Pseudomonas/sibling death point to CF. ✅ Correct if: the diagnosis were celiac disease (anti-tTG, villous atrophy, dermatitis herpetiformis).

eliminations.label: metronidazole — antibiotic for specific infections, not CF malabsorption
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: metronidazole treats anaerobes/protozoa (e.g., Giardia, C. difficile). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the diarrhea is malabsorptive (pancreatic insufficiency), not a treatable enteric infection. ✅ Correct if: the diarrhea were due to Giardia or C. difficile.

eliminations.label: octreotide — somatostatin analog, wrong target
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: octreotide (somatostatin analog) is used for secretory tumors/variceal bleeding/some secretory diarrheas. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't replace pancreatic enzymes or correct CF malabsorption. ✅ Correct if: the diarrhea were from a neuroendocrine tumor (e.g., VIPoma, carcinoid).

eliminations.label: sulfasalazine — for IBD, not CF
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: sulfasalazine is an anti-inflammatory used in inflammatory bowel disease. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this is malabsorption from pancreatic insufficiency, not colitis; it wouldn't restore nutrient absorption. ✅ Correct if: the diagnosis were ulcerative colitis/IBD.

reasoning: Recurrent Pseudomonas lung infections + steatorrhea + affected sibling = cystic fibrosis
CF causes exocrine pancreatic insufficiency → fat/protein malabsorption
Replacing pancreatic enzymes restores nutrient absorption
mechanism: Cystic fibrosis (autosomal recessive CFTR mutation, classically delta-F508) produces thick, viscous secretions that obstruct pancreatic ducts, destroying exocrine pancreas and causing enzyme deficiency. The result is malabsorption of fat and protein with steatorrhea, failure to thrive, and fat-soluble vitamin (A, D, E, K) deficiency. Oral pancreatic enzyme replacement (lipase, protease, amylase) restores digestion/absorption and improves growth; airway disease is managed separately.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid GI/Genetics > cystic fibrosis: CFTR (delta-F508), thick secretions, recurrent sinopulmonary infections (S. aureus, Pseudomonas), pancreatic insufficiency (steatorrhea, ADEK deficiency), diagnosis (sweat chloride), enzyme replacement.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Diagnose CF from the triad (Pseudomonas lungs + malabsorption + sibling death), then treat the GI problem at its cause: replace the missing pancreatic enzymes.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on CFTR, pancreatic insufficiency, steatorrhea, fat-soluble vitamins (ADEK), failure to thrive? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Gluten-free (celiac) and sulfasalazine (IBD) and antibiotics are GI-diarrhea baits — but CF malabsorption is from pancreatic insufficiency, fixed by pancreatic enzyme replacement.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The reference is the cystic-fibrosis features table (pathogenesis, clinical manifestations, diagnosis). Tie pancreatic insufficiency to enzyme replacement therapy.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.back: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency → steatorrhea, failure to thrive
S. aureus (early), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (later), B. cepacia
Elevated sweat chloride; CFTR genetic testing

firstAid.detail: GI/Genetics > cystic fibrosis: pancreatic insufficiency, steatorrhea, ADEK deficiency, pancreatic enzyme replacement; CFTR/delta-F508, sweat chloride.
firstAid.location: GI/Genetics > cystic fibrosis: pancreatic insufficiency, steatorrhea, ADEK deficiency, pancreatic enzyme replacement; CFTR/delta-F508, sweat chloride.

firstAid.detail: Pulmonary/Micro > CF airway disease: thick mucus, recurrent infections (S. aureus, Pseudomonas, Burkholderia), bronchiectasis.
firstAid.location: Pulmonary/Micro > CF airway disease: thick mucus, recurrent infections (S. aureus, Pseudomonas, Burkholderia), bronchiectasis.

firstAid.detail: GI > celiac (gluten-free), IBD (sulfasalazine), infectious diarrhea (metronidazole), secretory tumors (octreotide) — to contrast.
firstAid.location: GI > celiac (gluten-free), IBD (sulfasalazine), infectious diarrhea (metronidazole), secretory tumors (octreotide) — to contrast.

memoryHook: CF clogs the pancreatic ducts → no enzymes → fatty diarrhea & poor growth; give back the LIPASE (pancreatic enzymes) to absorb food.
